notes.claim: In 1905, the Japanese government incorporated Takeshima into Shimane Prefecture.
notes.context: On January 28, 1905, the Japanese government placed Takeshima under the jurisdiction of Okishima Tsukasa, Shimane Prefecture, by a cabinet decision. Based on the legal doctrine of first possession of land without title under international law. South Korea claims that the annexation was part of a military invasion carried out during the Russo-Japanese War (1904-05).

sources.title: Ministry of Foreign Affairs’ position regarding Takeshima
sources.publisher: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan

notes.claim: In the Rusk letter, the United States expressed the view that ``Takeshima is Japanese territory.''
notes.context: On August 10, 1951, in a letter from Assistant Secretary of State Dean Rusk to the Korean Ambassador, the United States notified South Korea of its opinion that Takeshima (Dokdo) was not included in the territory renounced by Japan under the San Francisco Treaty. However, South Korea counters that this letter is not public and is not an official interpretation of the treaty.

sources.title: Rusk Letter (1951)
sources.publisher: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan (cited)

notes.claim: In 1952, South Korea established the “Syngman Rhee Line” and included Takeshima.
notes.context: On January 18, 1952, South Korean President Syngman Rhee issued the ``President's Declaration on the Sovereignty of the Maritime Adjacent to the Republic of Korea,'' unilaterally establishing extensive maritime jurisdiction. Takeshima was included within this line. The Japanese government protests this as a unilateral measure that violates international law. Although the Syngman Rhee Line was abolished in 1965 with the Japan-Korea Basic Treaty, effective control of Takeshima continues by South Korea.

sources.title: History of the Takeshima issue
sources.publisher: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan

sources.title: Dokdo/Takeshima - Korea's Position
sources.publisher: Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Republic of Korea

notes.claim: Japan proposes to refer the case to the International Court of Justice (ICJ), but South Korea rejects it
notes.context: The Japanese government proposed referral to the ICJ three times: in 1954, 1962, and 2012. South Korea rejected both proposals, stating that ``Dokdo is South Korean territory and there is no territorial dispute.'' The ICJ's jurisdiction requires the consent of both parties, so if one party refuses, the case will not be successful.

sources.title: Proposal to refer the territorial rights of Takeshima to the ICJ
sources.publisher: Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Japan
